activity.title: Attorney General Bonta Opposes Trump Administration's Proposed Slashing of Healthcare AI Transparency and Bias Protections
activity.raw_text: Proposed rule would eliminate one of the most significant guardrails currently in place on a federal level for the use of AI in healthcare OAKLAND — California Attorney General Rob Bonta today announced that he sent a letter to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) opposing a proposed rule that would roll back regulations that help ensure technology used by healthcare providers is safe, effective, and deployed without reinforcing unjust racial bias. The proposed rule at issue — entitled "Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity" — would remove certification criteria requiring that model cards accompany health products that use artificial intelligence (AI).

_verify_phrases: remove certification criteria requiring that model cards accompany health products

source_entity.name: California Attorney General
source_entity.description: Most powerful sub-federal AI enforcer. Bonta's office issued 2025 AI advisories under existing California consumer-protection and civil-rights statutes — effectively a de-facto state AI regime via enforcement, independent of any new AI bill.

awareness.reasoning: This is a direct action by the user's own office (source_entity_name: California Attorney General) opposing the Trump Administration's proposed rollback of healthcare AI transparency and bias protections — squarely on the user's tracked topics of audit_requirements and preemption_state_federal. As a STATE_AG_ACTION from ca-ag, the office should already be coordinating internally and tracking the federal counterpart.
recommended_actions: Confirm internal coordination with the AG team that authored this opposition — ensure messaging aligns with ongoing post-SB1047 preemption posture.
Identify the underlying federal proposal being opposed and add to the federal preemption tracker — needed to support follow-on comments or multistate action.

activity.title: SB 1000 introduced — California AI Transparency Act amendments (urgency)
activity.raw_text: Existing law, the California AI Transparency Act, beginning August 2, 2026, generally regulates provenance data disclosure in content generated by artificial intelligence (AI), including by requiring a covered provider to make available an AI detection tool at no cost to the user that meets ... The Legislature finds and declares all of the following: (a) Establishing an ecosystem of information online that allows consumers to verify the provenance of content, including, but not limited to, whether or not it was generated or modified by a generative artificial intelligence (AI) system is paramount to combating misinformation and disinformation

_verify_phrases: paramount to combating misinformation and disinformation

source_entity.name: California Legislature
source_entity.description: Most prolific state AI lawmaking body. SB-1047 (Wiener) frontier-safety bill, vetoed in 2024, set the template for state-level catastrophic-risk regulation. Continues to push AB-2013 training-data transparency, deepfake/election laws, and labor-AI bills, making CA the de-facto US floor on AI rules.

awareness.reasoning: SB 1000 is an urgency amendment to the California AI Transparency Act introduced in the ca-legislature (a watched entity), directly implicating the user's high-weighted topics state_ai_legislation (0.9) and likely audit_requirements/content_provenance. Urgency status means accelerated effective date, increasing the AG office's enforcement-readiness stakes.
recommended_actions: Assign legislative affairs to read SB 1000 text and flag enforcement implications — urgency bills require fast AG-office positioning.
Coordinate with the bill's author on enforcement authority and remedies — ensures the AG can operationalize any new transparency obligations.

activity.title: AB 1898 introduced — Workplace artificial intelligence tools
activity.raw_text: An act to add Part 5.9 (commencing with Section 1600) to Division 2 of the Labor Code, relating to employment. AB 1898, as amended, Schultz. Workplace artificial intelligence tools. Existing law establishes the Division of Labor Standards Enforcement within the Department of Industrial Relations to administer and enforce various laws relating to employment and working conditions. This bill would require an employer to provide a written notice to a worker that a workplace AI tool, as defined, was used to assist the employer in making employment-related decisions or to surveil workers in the workplace.

_verify_phrases: a workplace AI tool, as defined, was used to assist the employer

activity.title: United States of America et al. v. RealPage, Inc. et al. Proposed Final Judgment and Competitive Impact Statement
activity.raw_text: United States of America et al. v. RealPage, Inc. et al. Proposed Final Judgment and Competitive Impact Statement

enrichment.summary: The DOJ Antitrust Division published a Proposed Final Judgment and Competitive Impact Statement in United States of America et al. v. RealPage, Inc. et al. This filing reflects the government's proposed resolution of its antitrust case against RealPage, a company that provides algorithmic rent-setting software used by landlords. The document outlines proposed remedies and assesses their competitive impact, as required under the Antitrust Procedures and Penalties Act before a consent decree can be finalized.

source_entity.name: DOJ Antitrust Division
source_entity.description: Investigates AI-market concentration with FTC under a jurisdictional split (DOJ took Nvidia, Microsoft; FTC took OpenAI, Anthropic, Google). Live merger reviews shape compute-market structure; Google search remedies trial sets precedent for AI-product distribution mandates.

awareness.reasoning: DOJ Antitrust's Proposed Final Judgment in the RealPage algorithmic rent-setting case directly implicates the user's antitrust_concentration topic and is a new_position federal proposal from a watched entity (doj-antitrust). California has been an active co-plaintiff in algorithmic-pricing matters, so the CA AG office likely has equities in the proposed remedies and Tunney Act process.
recommended_actions: Confirm whether CA is a co-plaintiff state on this Proposed Final Judgment and review remedy terms — algorithmic pricing aligns with CA AG consumer protection equities.
Coordinate with the AG's antitrust section on whether to file Tunney Act comments — materiality.novelty is 'new_position' on remedy scope.

activity.title: Attorney General Bonta Calls on Congress to Stop Government Mass Surveillance of Americans
activity.raw_text: OAKLAND — California Attorney General Rob Bonta today, alongside a coalition of 17 attorneys general, called on Congress to take immediate action to halt federal agencies' use of commercially purchased data and artificial intelligence tools that enable mass surveillance of Americans without judicial, legislative, or public oversight. The letter calls on Congress to close the data‑broker loophole, require warrants for federal access to Americans' digital data, prevent domestic surveillance via foreign intelligence laws, mandate deletion of unlawfully collected information and related AI models, and establish nationwide transparency and accountability standards for data brokers.

_verify_phrases: mandate deletion of unlawfully collected information and related AI models

source_entity.name: California Attorney General
source_entity.description: Most powerful sub-federal AI enforcer. Bonta's office issued 2025 AI advisories under existing California consumer-protection and civil-rights statutes — effectively a de-facto state AI regime via enforcement, independent of any new AI bill.

awareness.reasoning: This is a direct action by the user's own office — California Attorney General — calling on Congress on government mass surveillance. While it does not explicitly map to AI consumer protection, bias audits, or deepfakes, it is a watched-entity (ca-ag) public position that staff in the AG's office should be aware of for internal alignment.
recommended_actions: Circulate AG Bonta's surveillance statement to the AI/consumer protection team — ensures internal messaging consistency on federal-state privacy posture.

The attached Analysis of Proposed Consent Order to Aid Public Comment describes both the allegations in the complaint and the terms of the consent order--embodied in the consent agreement--that would settle these allegations.

activity.title: Rule Concerning the Use of Prenotification Negative Option Plans
activity.raw_text:
Rule Concerning the Use of Prenotification Negative Option Plans

The Federal Trade Commission ("FTC" or "Commission") seeks public comment on the need for amendments to the Commission's "Rule Concerning the Use of Prenotification Negative Option Plans" (i.e., "Negative Option Rule" or "Rule") to help consumers avoid recurring payments for products and services they did not intend to order and to allow them to cancel such payments without unwarranted obstacles.

Advance notice of proposed rulemaking; request for public comment.

payload.action: Advance notice of proposed rulemaking; request for public comment.

enrichment.summary: The FTC issued an advance notice of proposed rulemaking (ANPRM) seeking public comment on potential amendments to its Prenotification Negative Option Rule. The Rule governs subscription and continuity programs where consumers must affirmatively cancel to avoid recurring charges. The FTC is exploring changes to help consumers avoid unintended recurring payments and to reduce obstacles to cancellation.
enrichment.stance_quote: to help consumers avoid recurring payments for products and services they did not intend to order and to allow them to cancel such payments without unwarranted obstacles

Post-2025 leadership shift partially reorients posture.

activity.title: Petition for Rulemaking of Andrew Gonzalez
activity.raw_text:
Petition for Rulemaking of Andrew Gonzalez

Please take notice that the Federal Trade Commission ("Commission") received a petition for rulemaking from Andrew Gonzalez and has published that petition online at https://www.regulations.gov. The Commission invites written comments concerning the petition. Publication of this petition is pursuant to the Commission's Rules of Practice and Procedure and does not affect the legal status of the petition or its final disposition.

Receipt of petition; request for comment.

activity.title: Rule on Unfair or Deceptive Fees in Online Food Delivery Services
activity.raw_text:
Rule on Unfair or Deceptive Fees in Online Food Delivery Services

The Federal Trade Commission ("Commission") proposes to commence a rulemaking proceeding to address certain unfair or deceptive acts or practices relating to fees and charges for food and grocery items ordered through online delivery platforms. The Commission is soliciting written comment, data, and argument concerning the need for such a rulemaking to prevent persons, entities, and organizations from engaging in such unfair or deceptive acts or practices.

Advance notice of proposed rulemaking; request for public comment.

payload.action: Advance notice of proposed rulemaking; request for public comment.

enrichment.summary: The FTC issued an advance notice of proposed rulemaking (ANPRM) to address unfair or deceptive fees and charges on online food and grocery delivery platforms. The Commission is soliciting public comments, data, and arguments on whether a formal rulemaking is needed. The notice targets practices by persons, entities, and organizations operating online delivery platforms. No specific rules have been proposed yet; this is a preliminary step to gather information before any rulemaking.
enrichment.stance_quote: proposes to commence a rulemaking proceeding to address certain unfair or deceptive acts or practices relating to fees and charges for food and grocery items ordered through online delivery platforms
